column1: What is the difference between functional and non-functional requirements?
column2: Functional requirements describe what users or clients can do; non-functional requirements describe system qualities like latency, scale, availability, durability, security, and compliance.

column1: Why should non-functional requirements be quantified when possible?
column2: A concrete target identifies the part of the system that needs special design attention and gives you something to evaluate tradeoffs against.

column1: If search must return results in under 500ms, what kind of requirement is that?
column2: A quantified latency NFR because it names both the operation and the target response time.

column1: When should capacity estimates change a design?
column2: When the numbers decide an architectural question, such as whether one machine, one heap, one cache, or one database can handle the load.

column1: Why might a TopK trending system require capacity estimation?
column2: The number of tracked topics can determine whether a single in-memory data structure is enough or the computation must be sharded.

column1: What are core entities in a system design?
column2: The central actors, nouns, and resources exchanged through APIs and persisted in the data model.

column1: Why start with a simple complete architecture before adding caches, queues, and shards?
column2: A simple design proves the core data flow works; extra components should be added to satisfy specific scale, latency, reliability, or consistency needs.

column1: What communication protocol is the default for most systems in the note?
column2: HTTP over TCP because it is well understood, works broadly, and handles most common use cases.

column1: How do SSE and WebSockets differ?
column2: SSE is server-to-client only; WebSockets are bidirectional and support frequent client-to-server messages.
column3: system-design networking realtime sse websocket sdiah-009

column1: How does SSE deliver updates?
column2: The client opens an HTTP connection, then the server pushes data down that connection; the client cannot send additional data over the same SSE connection.

column1: When are WebSockets necessary instead of SSE?
column2: When clients need to push data back to the server frequently over the same realtime connection.

column1: Why do stateful realtime connections require extra operational planning?
column2: A server failure can drop thousands of active connections, and load balancers need to preserve long-lived connection behavior.
column3: system-design networking realtime load-balancing reliability sdiah-012

column1: When is gRPC a good fit?
column2: Internal service-to-service communication where performance is critical.

column1: Why is gRPC usually not chosen for public browser-facing APIs?
column2: Browsers do not typically support gRPC natively as conveniently as REST or JSON-over-HTTP APIs.

column1: What is the main difference between L4 and L7 load balancers?
column2: L4 balances TCP-level connections; L7 can inspect HTTP details and route based on request content.

column1: Which load balancer layer is commonly used for persistent WebSocket connections?
column2: L4, because WebSockets maintain persistent TCP connections.

column1: Why do CDNs reduce geography-driven latency?
column2: They serve cacheable content from edge locations closer to users, reducing the physical distance a request travels.

column1: What latency floor does the note give for a New York to London request?
column2: Around 80ms before application processing, due to the speed of light through fiber.

column1: What does REST map in API design?
column2: Resources to URLs and HTTP methods to operations on those resources.

column1: What API style is the usual default unless a specific reason says otherwise?
column2: REST.

column1: When is GraphQL a reasonable API choice?
column2: When clients have diverse data needs and avoiding over-fetching or under-fetching is important.

column1: When is RPC or gRPC a good interface choice?
column2: Action-oriented internal service calls where performance is critical.
column3: system-design api-design rpc grpc microservices sdiah-022

column1: When should realtime APIs add WebSockets or SSE?
column2: When the system needs server-pushed realtime behavior beyond the core request-response API.
column3: system-design api-design realtime websocket sse sdiah-023

column1: Why should a user ID usually come from the auth token instead of the request body?
column2: Request bodies can be spoofed; identity should come from authenticated context such as a JWT.

column1: Why use plural resource names in REST endpoints?
column2: They represent collections of resources, such as tweets rather than tweet.

column1: When is cursor-based pagination better than offset-based pagination?
column2: For realtime or frequently changing data where inserts can shift offsets and cause unstable pages.

column1: When is offset-based pagination acceptable?
column2: For most simpler cases where result sets are not changing in ways that make offsets unstable.

column1: What auth mechanisms does the note suggest for user sessions and service-to-service calls?
column2: JWTs for user sessions and API keys for service-to-service calls.

column1: When should rate limiting be part of an API design?
column2: When bots, abuse, or high request volume could hammer the system.

column1: Why does data modeling affect system behavior downstream?
column2: Stored data shape affects query performance, scalability, consistency, and maintenance complexity.

column1: When are relational databases a strong fit?
column2: Structured data with clear relationships and a need for strong consistency.

column1: What is normalization?
column2: Splitting data across related tables to avoid duplication and preserve consistency.

column1: What is denormalization?
column2: Duplicating data to avoid joins and speed reads, at the cost of harder updates and consistency maintenance.

column1: What is the recommended default around normalization and denormalization?
column2: Start with a normalized model, then denormalize specific hot read paths when there is a clear performance reason.

column1: When do NoSQL databases shine?
column2: Flexible schemas or horizontal scaling across many servers without complex joins.

column1: In DynamoDB, why must partition and sort keys be designed around access patterns?
column2: They make chosen queries efficient, but unplanned queries may require scans or additional indexes.

column1: What does schema-on-read imply for flexible data models?
column2: Schema flexibility does not remove modeling complexity; it can move validation and interpretation to read time.

column1: What is the purpose of a database index?
column2: To make queries fast by avoiding full scans over every row.

column1: What happens if you look up users by email without an index?
column2: The database may scan every row in the user table.

column1: What query types do B-tree indexes support well?
column2: Exact lookups and range queries because data is kept sorted in a tree structure.

column1: What is the limitation of hash indexes compared with B-tree indexes?
column2: Hash indexes are fast for exact matches but cannot do range queries.

column1: How should you decide which fields to index?
column2: Index fields used by frequent query patterns, such as email for login or city and date for event search.

column1: What is a compound index useful for?
column2: Queries that filter or sort by multiple fields, such as city and date together.

column1: When might you need an external indexing system?
column2: When specialized search or geospatial needs exceed what the primary database supports well.

column1: What consistency tradeoff appears when an external search index syncs via CDC?
column2: The search index can lag behind the primary database and return slightly stale results.
column3: system-design indexing search consistency cdc sdiah-045

column1: Why use a cache in a read-heavy system?
column2: To keep frequently accessed data in fast memory so most reads can skip the database.

column1: What performance difference does the note give between Redis and a typical DB query?
column2: Redis cache access is around 1ms, versus roughly 20-50ms for a typical DB query.

column1: How does cache-aside work on a read?
column2: Check the cache first; on miss, query the database, store the result with a TTL, and return it.

column1: What is cache invalidation?
column2: Updating or deleting cached data when backing data changes so future reads do not return stale data.

column1: What are two basic cache invalidation strategies from the note?
column2: Invalidate immediately after writes, or use short TTLs and accept bounded staleness.

column1: What is a cache stampede?
column2: A cache failure or mass miss causes many requests to hit the database at once, overwhelming downstream capacity.

column1: How can a cache failure cause user requests to time out before the database process crashes?
column2: App connection pools saturate, new requests queue waiting for connections, and requests fail after timeouts.

column1: Why can autoscaling make a cache stampede worse for the database?
column2: More app instances can create many more possible DB connections, exceeding DB limits or wasting DB work on connection management.

column1: How can overloaded DB queries create a feedback loop during a stampede?
column2: Slow queries hold connections longer, pools free connections more slowly, app requests queue longer, and latency worsens.

column1: What mitigations can reduce cache-stampede damage?
column2: Backpressure, bounded connection pools, short timeouts, circuit breakers, request coalescing, stale serving, rate limits, and avoiding retry storms.

column1: Why is "cache everything" a bad rule?
column2: Caches help most when data is read often and changes infrequently; otherwise they add latency and invalidation complexity.

column1: How is CDN caching different from application-data caching with Redis?
column2: CDNs cache static or edge-suitable content near users; Redis usually caches core application data in memory.

column1: When does sharding become relevant?
column2: When a single database cannot handle storage, write throughput, or read throughput even with replicas.

column1: Why is the shard key the most important sharding decision?
column2: It determines data distribution, query locality, hot spots, and cross-shard access patterns.

column1: What is the tradeoff of sharding a user-centric app by user_id?
column2: User-scoped queries are fast on one shard, but global queries like trending posts require hitting many shards and aggregating.

column1: How does hash-based sharding typically assign data to shards?
column2: Hash the shard key and map it to a shard, often using modulo over the shard count.

column1: Why can range-based sharding create hot spots?
column2: One range can receive disproportionate traffic compared with other ranges.

column1: What is the tradeoff of directory-based sharding?
column2: It is flexible, but every request depends on a lookup table and pays extra latency.

column1: What costs does premature sharding introduce?
column2: Cross-shard transactions, hot spots, painful resharding, more operational complexity, and harder query patterns.

column1: If a system has 10k writes per second and 100GB of data, what does the note imply about sharding?
column2: That alone does not justify sharding; a well-tuned single database with read replicas may still be enough.

column1: Why are cross-shard transactions difficult?
column2: They require coordinating updates across shards, often needing distributed transactions or sagas, which are complex and slow.

column1: What is a sharding hot spot?
column2: One shard receives disproportionate load, often because a popular key or user concentrates traffic.

column1: What problem does consistent hashing solve compared with hash(key) mod N?
column2: Adding or removing servers changes N, causing many keys to remap and forcing large data movement.

column1: How does consistent hashing assign keys to servers?
column2: Servers and keys are placed on a virtual ring; each key belongs to the next server encountered clockwise.

column1: What moves when a server is added in consistent hashing?
column2: Only keys between the new server and the previous counter-clockwise server need to move.

column1: What moves when a server is removed in consistent hashing?
column2: Only that server's keys move to the next server on the ring; other keys stay put.

column1: In a 10-server cluster, how much data movement does the note contrast for modulo hashing vs consistent hashing after adding one server?
column2: Modulo hashing may move about 90 percent of data; consistent hashing moves about 10 percent.

column1: What are the three CAP properties?
column2: Consistency, availability, and partition tolerance.

column1: In practice, what CAP choice are you usually making?
column2: Choosing between consistency and availability when a network partition occurs.

column1: What happens if a system chooses consistency during a partition?
column2: Some nodes refuse requests rather than returning potentially stale data.

column1: What happens if a system chooses availability during a partition?
column2: Nodes keep serving requests, but different nodes may temporarily return different data until the partition heals.

column1: When is eventual consistency usually acceptable?
column2: When users can tolerate slightly stale data, such as feeds, recommendations, or analytics dashboards.

column1: When does strong consistency matter most?
column2: When stale data causes real business harm, such as fraud, oversold inventory, or double-booked limited resources.

column1: In e-commerce, which data can be eventually consistent and which likely needs strong consistency?
column2: Product descriptions and reviews can be eventually consistent; inventory counts and order processing need stronger consistency.

column1: What does PACELC add beyond CAP?
column2: During a partition choose availability or consistency; otherwise choose latency or consistency.

column1: Why does strong consistency add latency even when the network is healthy?
column2: Nodes need to coordinate before responding.

column1: What rough latency hierarchy should you remember from the note?
column2: Memory access is nanoseconds, SSD reads are microseconds, datacenter calls are 1-10ms, and cross-continent calls are tens to hundreds of ms.

column1: Where should core structured data usually live when blobs are involved?
column2: In a core database such as Postgres or DynamoDB, with pointers to blobs stored in object storage.

column1: What is a database transaction?
column2: A group of operations treated as one atomic unit that either succeeds together or fails together.

column1: Why can joins become a design concern?
column2: Joins can become a performance bottleneck, especially on hot paths or large datasets.

column1: What database models fall under NoSQL in the note?
column2: Key-value, document, column-family, and graph databases.

column1: Why might Cassandra be chosen?
column2: It can fit write-heavy workloads because of its append-only storage model, though with functionality tradeoffs.
column3: system-design databases nosql cassandra writes sdiah-087

column1: How do NoSQL systems commonly scale horizontally?
column2: By using consistent hashing or sharding to distribute data across many servers.
column3: system-design databases nosql scaling sharding sdiah-088

column1: When should you use blob storage instead of a traditional database?
column2: For large unstructured files such as images, videos, documents, and other file blobs.

column1: Why should large blobs usually not be stored directly in the core database?
column2: It is expensive and inefficient compared with object storage such as S3 or Google Cloud Storage.

column1: What should the core database store when blobs live in object storage?
column2: Metadata and pointers such as object URLs or keys, not the large file bytes.

column1: What is a presigned URL used for?
column2: Granting temporary scoped access so clients can upload or download directly without proxying file bytes through app servers.

column1: What are the upload-path steps for large blobs?
column2: App authorizes and creates pending metadata, returns a presigned upload URL, client uploads to object storage, app verifies the object, then marks it ready.

column1: What are the read-path steps for private large blobs behind a CDN?
column2: Client requests access, app returns a short-lived signed CDN URL, CDN validates it, then serves from cache or fetches from object storage origin.

column1: Why chunk large file uploads?
column2: Chunking enables parallel upload and resume after partial failure.

column1: How does object storage often work with a CDN?
column2: Object storage acts as the origin, and the CDN caches files in edge locations for faster global downloads.

column1: Why is naive SQL substring search often slow?
column2: Searching text with a contains pattern can require a full table scan.

column1: What is an inverted index?
column2: A mapping from terms to the documents that contain them, enabling fast full-text lookup.

column1: What is tokenization in search indexing?
column2: Breaking text into individual words or tokens so they can be mapped in an inverted index.

column1: What is stemming in search?
column2: Reducing words to root forms so different forms of a word can match.

column1: What is fuzzy search?
column2: Finding results similar to a given search term instead of requiring exact matches.

column1: How do search-optimized databases scale?
column2: By adding more nodes and sharding data across those nodes.

column1: What does an API gateway do in a microservice architecture?
column2: It sits in front of backend services, routes requests, and handles cross-cutting concerns like auth, rate limiting, and logging.

column1: What are three ways identity can be propagated from an API gateway to services?
column2: Forward the original JWT, inject trusted identity headers, or exchange for internal service-scoped tokens.

column1: Why are trusted identity headers dangerous if services are reachable directly?
column2: Clients could spoof them unless the gateway strips incoming identity headers and services only accept trusted internal traffic.

column1: Why can internal service tokens be cleaner than forwarding an external JWT?
column2: They support least privilege and service-specific audiences, but require more infrastructure and policy design.
column3: system-design api-gateway auth security microservices sdiah-106

column1: What is the mental model for authentication vs authorization in microservices?
column2: Authentication can happen at the edge; authorization often belongs in the service that owns the resource.

column1: Why use a load balancer?
column2: To distribute traffic across multiple machines and avoid overloading a single instance.

column1: What is the simple rule for choosing L4 vs L7 load balancers?
column2: Use L4 for persistent connections like WebSockets; use L7 when HTTP-aware routing flexibility matters.

column1: Does each service pool necessarily need its own physical load balancer?
column2: No. Service pools can have separate logical target groups behind one shared L7 load balancer.

column1: When might different service pools need separate load balancers?
column2: Separate public or private exposure, security boundaries, protocols, scaling limits, ownership, cost isolation, or blast-radius reduction.

column1: What routing decisions can an L7 load balancer make that L4 cannot?
column2: It can route by host, path, HTTP method, headers, cookies, query params, and sometimes request body.

column1: What does an L4 load balancer mostly see?
column2: Source IP, destination IP, port, and TCP or UDP connection metadata.

column1: What are the two main purposes of message queues in the note?
column2: Buffer bursty traffic and distribute work across a system.

column1: How do queues decouple producers and consumers?
column2: Producers enqueue work and return; consumers process messages at their own pace, allowing independent scaling.

column1: If 1,000 requests arrive but workers handle 200 per second, what does a queue do?
column2: It holds excess requests so they wait instead of being immediately dropped, assuming backlog is acceptable.

column1: Why should queues be used carefully in synchronous workloads?
column2: They can violate strict latency requirements such as sub-500ms responses.

column1: What is a dead letter queue?
column2: A place to store messages that cannot be processed after retries, useful for debugging and auditing.

column1: What retry behavior do many queues provide?
column2: They redeliver failed messages a configured number of times, often with configurable delay and maximum attempts.

column1: Why do queues use partitions?
column2: To scale message storage and processing across servers and worker groups.

column1: Why does a queue partition key matter?
column2: It keeps related messages on the same partition when ordering or locality matters.

column1: What is the queue backpressure problem?
column2: A queue can hide that producers exceed consumer capacity, causing backlog to grow indefinitely.

column1: What can backpressure do when a queue is overwhelmed?
column2: Slow or reject new messages so the queue does not become an unbounded bottleneck.

column1: How does a pull-based queue work?
column2: Workers ask the queue for messages, process them, then acknowledge or delete them.

column1: What does an SQS visibility timeout do?
column2: It temporarily hides a received message from other workers; if not deleted in time, it becomes visible again.

column1: How does a push-based queue work?
column2: A subscribed worker keeps a connection open and the broker sends messages, with acknowledgements and flow control.

column1: What does delivery mean in a queue system?
column2: The broker handed a message to a consumer or made it available under a lease and acknowledgement contract.

column1: Why are messages redelivered?
column2: If a consumer fails before acknowledging, the broker makes the message available for processing again.

column1: How are streams different from ordinary message queues?
column2: Streams retain data for a configurable period, allowing consumers to reread from positions or times.

column1: When are streams a good choice?
column2: Large-scale realtime event processing, event sourcing, or multiple consumers reading the same event feed.

column1: What is event sourcing?
column2: Storing state changes as a sequence of events that can be replayed to reconstruct application state.

column1: Why do streams support multiple consumer groups?
column2: Different consumers can independently process the same stream for different purposes.

column1: How do stream partitions help scaling?
column2: Each partition can be processed by a different consumer, enabling horizontal scaling.

column1: Why do streams replicate data?
column2: To support fault tolerance across servers.

column1: What is windowing in stream processing?
column2: Grouping events by time or count to process batches such as hourly or daily aggregates.

column1: When are distributed locks useful?
column2: When a resource must be held across processes or systems for a short period, such as a ticket reservation.

column1: What property lets a key-value store implement a distributed lock?
column2: Atomic operations ensure only one process can acquire the lock at a time.

column1: Why should distributed locks expire?
column2: Expiry prevents locks from staying stuck forever if the holder crashes or is killed.

column1: What is lock granularity?
column2: The scope of what the lock protects, such as one ticket versus a group of seats.

column1: What is a deadlock?
column2: A situation where two or more processes wait for each other to release locks.

column1: When should database transactions be preferred over distributed locks?
column2: When protecting database state, because the database can enforce correctness directly.

column1: When is Redis plus Redlock a reasonable fit?
column2: Fast, approximate, short-lived coordination where occasional duplicate work is tolerable.

column1: What should you use for real service coordination or leader election?
column2: Systems like etcd, ZooKeeper, Consul, or Kubernetes Lease.

column1: What are fencing tokens for?
column2: Blocking stale lock holders when correctness matters.

column1: What are common uses for distributed caches?
column2: Saving expensive aggregates, reducing database queries, and speeding up expensive query results.

column1: What is a cache eviction policy?
column2: A rule for deciding which cached items to remove when the cache is full.

column1: How do LRU, FIFO, and LFU eviction differ?
column2: LRU evicts least recently used items, FIFO evicts oldest inserted items, and LFU evicts least frequently used items.

column1: How do write-through, write-around, and write-back caches differ?
column2: Write-through writes cache and datastore together; write-around bypasses cache on writes; write-back writes cache first and datastore later.

column1: What is the tradeoff of write-through caching?
column2: It improves consistency between cache and datastore but can slow write operations.

column1: What is the tradeoff of write-around caching?
column2: It minimizes cache pollution but can make later reads slower because fresh data is not in cache.

column1: What is the main risk of write-back caching?
column2: Data can be lost if the cache fails before async persistence to the datastore completes.

column1: Why can Redis data structures matter in a design?
column2: Redis is not just key-value; structures like sorted sets can model ranked or ordered data efficiently.

column1: How do Redis and Memcached differ in the note?
column2: Redis supports many data structures; Memcached is a simpler key-value store for strings and binary objects.

column1: What is a CDN?
column2: A geographically distributed cache that serves content from locations closer to users.

column1: What happens on a CDN cache miss?
column2: The CDN fetches content from the origin server, caches it, and returns it to the user.

column1: What is an origin server in a CDN setup?
column2: The backing server or storage system the CDN fetches from when content is not cached at the edge.

column1: What content are CDNs commonly used for?
column2: Static media assets like images and videos, and sometimes cacheable HTML or API responses.

column1: How can CDN cached content be expired or removed?
column2: With TTLs or cache invalidation mechanisms when content changes.
column3: system-design cdn caching ttl cache-invalidation sdiah-158

column1: What is the simplest starting protocol for realtime updates?
column2: HTTP polling, until requirements show it no longer meets the system's needs.

column1: When should you consider SSE or WebSockets?
column2: When chat, notifications, dashboards, or similar features require server-pushed realtime updates.

column1: Why are SSE and WebSocket systems trickier than polling?
column2: They involve persistent connections and more complex server-side infrastructure.

column1: What server-side pattern can decouple publishers and subscribers for realtime updates?
column2: Pub-sub systems.

column1: What is the long-running task pattern?
column2: Validate quickly, enqueue work, return a job ID, and let background workers process the task.

column1: What operations often fit the long-running task pattern?
column2: Video encoding, report generation, bulk operations, or tasks taking more than a few seconds.

column1: What benefits does the long-running task pattern provide?
column2: Fast user responses, independent scaling of web servers and workers, and fault isolation.

column1: Why can moving work behind a queue be a bad decision?
column2: If the work is short-running, synchronous processing may be much simpler and still meet latency needs.

column1: What problem does the contention pattern address?
column2: Multiple users trying to access the same resource simultaneously, risking race conditions or inconsistent state.

column1: What solution range exists for contention?
column2: Database transactions and locking, optimistic or pessimistic concurrency, distributed locks, 2PC, or queue-based serialization.

column1: Why start contention handling with single-database solutions when possible?
column2: They are simpler than distributed coordination and often provide the needed atomicity and consistency guarantees.

column1: What traffic bottleneck often appears first as applications grow?
column2: Read traffic, often starting around 10:1 reads to writes and growing to 100:1 or higher.

column1: In what order should you consider scaling reads?
column2: Optimize the database with indexes, denormalize hot paths if needed, then add horizontal scaling and caching.
column3: system-design scaling reads indexing caching sdiah-171

column1: What read-scaling issues should you watch for?
column2: Cache invalidation and eviction, read-replica lag, and hot keys.
column3: system-design scaling reads caching replication hotspots sdiah-172

column1: How can high write volume be addressed?
column2: Sharding or vertical partitioning, queues for bursts, load shedding, and batching.
column3: system-design scaling writes sharding queues sdiah-173

column1: What makes a good write partition key?
column2: It distributes load evenly while keeping related data together when possible.
column3: system-design scaling writes sharding partitions sdiah-174

column1: What is load shedding in write-heavy overload?
column2: Intentionally dropping or rejecting lower-priority writes so the system can preserve capacity for more important work.

column1: Why can batching improve write throughput?
column2: It reduces per-operation overhead by grouping multiple writes together.

column1: What is the core pattern for handling large files?
column2: Use direct client-to-object-storage transfers with presigned URLs and serve reads through a CDN.

column1: What state synchronization challenge exists with blobs?
column2: Database metadata can get out of sync with object storage if uploads fail or processing state is not updated.

column1: Why should uploads not route gigabytes through app servers?
column2: It wastes app-server bandwidth and compute; direct object-storage upload is more scalable.

column1: What are multi-step process patterns for?
column2: Coordinating complex business workflows across services, retries, failures, and external dependencies.

column1: When might Temporal or AWS Step Functions be appropriate?
column2: When durable workflow execution, state management, retries, recovery, and auditability matter.

column1: What is the key benefit of declarative workflow definitions?
column2: The workflow system manages execution state, retries, failure recovery, and audit trails instead of scattered manual logic.

column1: What index type is central to proximity-based services?
column2: Geospatial indexes.

column1: What technologies can support proximity queries?
column2: Postgres with PostGIS, Redis geospatial data types, or Elasticsearch with geo-queries.

column1: How do proximity services reduce the search space?
column2: They divide geographic areas into manageable regions and index entities within those regions.

column1: Why do proximity systems usually avoid global searches?
column2: Users usually need nearby local entities, so the system can limit queries to relevant regions.

column1: How might a video platform combine common patterns?
column2: Large blobs for uploads, long-running tasks for transcoding, realtime updates for progress, and workflows to coordinate the process.
column3: system-design common-patterns blob-storage workflows realtime sdiah-187

column1: How should you decide which patterns to use in a system?
column2: Match patterns to specific requirements and tradeoffs, start simple, and add complexity only when requirements demand it.
